coreJobStatement: Transform water, malt, hops, and yeast into packaged beer through controlled biochemical and physical processes
hierarchy: Process VN (L7 through L4) — what process steps and equipment compose the beer manufacturing chain
marketSize: ~$18–23B globally (brewery equipment, 2025); ~$600B global beer market [SRC: web_search]

vnUnits.name: Grain Silo & Storage
vnUnits.functionalJob: Bulk storage of base malt, specialty malts, and adjuncts (rice, corn, wheat). Temperature and moisture monitoring prevents spoilage.

vnUnits.name: Malt Mill
vnUnits.functionalJob: Crushes grain into grist, exposing starch granules for enzyme access during mashing. Roller gap setting is critical — too fine clogs the lauter; too coarse reduces yield.

vnUnits.name: Mash Tun / Mash Mixer
vnUnits.functionalJob: Vessel where grist is mixed with hot water ("liquor") at controlled temperatures (62–72 °C) to activate enzymes that convert starch to fermentable sugars. [SRC: web_search]

vnUnits.name: Lauter Tun
vnUnits.functionalJob: Separates sweet wort from spent grain. The grain bed acts as a natural filter. Sparging (rinsing) extracts residual sugars. Lauter efficiency determines brewhouse yield (typically 75–85%). [SRC: web_search]

vnUnits.name: Brew Kettle
vnUnits.functionalJob: Boils wort for 60–90 minutes. Purposes: hop isomerization (bitterness), protein coagulation (hot break), sterilization, and dimethyl sulfide (DMS) drive-off. Energy-intensive step (~35% of total brewery energy). [SRC: web_search]

vnUnits.name: Whirlpool
vnUnits.functionalJob: Separates trub (coagulated protein, hop debris) from clear wort using centrifugal settling. Tangential inlet creates vortex; solids collect in a cone at the centre. [SRC: web_search]

vnUnits.name: Wort Heat Exchanger
vnUnits.functionalJob: Rapidly cools wort from ~100 °C to fermentation temperature (10–20 °C) in minutes. Speed prevents contamination and DMS formation. Counter-flow plate heat exchanger is standard; cold liquor from CLT absorbs heat and is recovered as hot liquor in the HLT. [SRC: web_search]

vnUnits.name: Conical Fermenter / Unitank
vnUnits.functionalJob: Primary fermentation vessel. Cylindro-conical design (CCT) dominates — cone bottom allows yeast harvesting and CIP without transfer. Unitanks combine fermentation and conditioning in one vessel. Glycol-jacketed for precise temperature control (±0.5 °C). [SRC: web_search]

vnUnits.name: Yeast Management
vnUnits.functionalJob: Propagate, pitch, harvest, and store yeast. Healthy yeast at correct cell count (0.75–1.5 M cells/mL/°P) determines fermentation performance and flavour consistency.

vnUnits.name: Conditioning / Maturation Tank
vnUnits.functionalJob: Secondary maturation for flavour development, haze reduction, and natural carbonation. Lagers may condition 2–6 weeks at 0–2 °C (lagering). Some breweries use unitanks for combined fermentation + conditioning.

vnUnits.name: Beer Filtration
vnUnits.functionalJob: Removes yeast, haze-forming proteins, and particles. Not used for all styles (many craft ales are unfiltered). Technologies: plate-and-frame (DE), candle filter, cross-flow membrane.

vnUnits.name: Carbonation System
vnUnits.functionalJob: Injects CO2 to achieve target carbonation (2.2–2.8 vol for most styles). Inline carbonation stone downstream of filter, upstream of bright tank.

vnUnits.name: Bright Beer Tank (BBT)
vnUnits.functionalJob: Pressure-rated holding tank (0.5–2.0 bar) for filtered, carbonated beer ready for packaging. Buffer between cellar and packaging line.

vnUnits.name: Bottle Filling Line
vnUnits.functionalJob: Rinse, counter-pressure fill, crown, and label glass bottles. Typical speeds: 20–600 bottles/min depending on brewery scale.

vnUnits.name: Can Filling Line
vnUnits.functionalJob: Purge, fill, and seam aluminium cans. Fastest-growing packaging format in craft beer. Typical speeds: 30–2,000 cans/min. [SRC: web_search]

vnUnits.name: Keg Filling Line
vnUnits.functionalJob: Wash, sanitize, fill, and seal stainless steel kegs (1/6 bbl, 1/4 bbl, 1/2 bbl). Keg washing is critical — returning kegs carry residual beer and potential contaminants.

vnUnits.name: Pasteurizer
vnUnits.functionalJob: Heat-treats packaged beer (62–72 °C for 15–30 s flash; or tunnel at 60 °C for 20 min) for shelf stability. Required for unpasteurized beer destined for warm-chain distribution; most craft draft skips this step.

vnUnits.name: Glycol Chiller Unit
vnUnits.functionalJob: Central refrigeration package cooling the glycol/water reservoir. Compressor types: scroll (small craft), screw (mid-size), centrifugal (large). Sized to total cooling load (typically 5–500 kW). [SRC: web_search]

vnUnits.name: Glycol Distribution
vnUnits.functionalJob: Piping, pumps, and valves distributing glycol from chiller to all consuming vessels and back. Pipe sizing (typically DN20–DN50) must balance flow velocity and pressure drop.

vnUnits.name: Glycol Sensing & Control
vnUnits.functionalJob: Sensors monitoring glycol flow rate, temperature, and pressure at each cooling zone. Flow data enables per-fermenter energy accounting and early detection of jacket blockage or pump degradation. **MARQUARDT PRIMARY POSITION.** [SRC: web_search]

vnUnits.name: Glycol Reservoir
vnUnits.functionalJob: Insulated buffer tank holding glycol/water mixture. Provides thermal inertia and decouples chiller operation from instantaneous cooling demand.

vnUnits.name: CIP Station / Skid
vnUnits.functionalJob: Centralized chemical storage, heating, and pumping unit. Typically 2–4 tanks (caustic, acid, rinse water, recovered rinse) with a supply and return pump. [SRC: web_search]

vnUnits.name: CIP Distribution & Spray Devices
vnUnits.functionalJob: Piping, valves, and spray devices routing cleaning solution to every vessel, heat exchanger, and pipeline in the brewery.

vnUnits.name: CIP Sensing & Control
vnUnits.functionalJob: Sensors verifying that each CIP step meets time, temperature, flow rate, and chemical concentration targets. Flow rate verification ensures turbulent flow (Re > 30,000) for effective soil removal. **MARQUARDT SECONDARY POSITION.** [SRC: web_search]

vnUnits.name: Water Treatment System
vnUnits.functionalJob: Filters and adjusts municipal or well water to target brewing profiles. Technologies: carbon filtration (chlorine removal), reverse osmosis (demineralization), UV sterilization, mineral dosing.

vnUnits.name: Hot Liquor Tank (HLT)
vnUnits.functionalJob: Heated water storage (75–85 °C) for mashing and sparging. Receives pre-heated water recovered from wort cooling, reducing energy consumption. [SRC: web_search]

vnUnits.name: Cold Liquor Tank (CLT)
vnUnits.functionalJob: Chilled water storage (1–4 °C) for wort cooling via the plate heat exchanger. May be glycol-cooled or filled with pre-chilled municipal water overnight. [SRC: web_search]

vnUnits.name: Water Flow Metering
vnUnits.functionalJob: Measures water intake (municipal meter), distribution to brewhouse, CIP, and packaging. Data drives water-use efficiency reporting (hL water / hL beer). **MARQUARDT TERTIARY POSITION** — water is the ideal medium for ultrasonic transit-time flow measurement.

vnUnits.name: Steam / Hot Water Generation
vnUnits.functionalJob: Provides process heat for brewhouse (kettle boil), CIP heating, and pasteurization. Natural gas fire-tube boilers dominate; electric boilers emerging in smaller craft facilities.

vnUnits.name: Compressed Air System
vnUnits.functionalJob: Plant air for pneumatic valve actuation, packaging equipment, and grain conveying. Sterile-filtered air for wort aeration.

vnUnits.name: CO2 Recovery & Distribution
vnUnits.functionalJob: Captures CO2 generated during fermentation, purifies, liquefies, and stores for reuse in carbonation, packaging purge, and tank blanketing. Reduces purchased CO2 costs by 50–80% in mid-to-large breweries.

vnUnits.name: Quality Assurance Laboratory
vnUnits.functionalJob: In-process and finished-product testing ensuring beer meets specifications. Tests: original gravity, alcohol by volume, pH, colour (SRM/EBC), bitterness (IBU), dissolved oxygen, microbiological stability.

vnUnits.name: Wastewater Treatment
vnUnits.functionalJob: Brewery effluent (BOD 2,000–6,000 mg/L) must be treated before municipal discharge. On-site treatment reduces surcharges and environmental impact.
